text: The surgeon applies tape to both buttocks near the edges of the cleft markings and draws them apart, opening the cleft like a fan with the apex serving as the hinge.

The surgeon pulls the tape laterally and upwards to open the cleft near the anus, revealing an inverted V shape formed by the previously parallel inked lines marking the cleft edges. This maneuver is crucial for visualizing and mobilizing the skin around open wounds near the anus.

The surgeon shaves the cleft, removing residual hair from the perianal skin. Despite appearing flat, the cleft is naturally deep, and the surgical goal is to make it shallower and flatter by removing skin.

The surgeon excises skin from one side of the cleft and drapes skin from the opposite side across the midline to cover the denuded area. The incision is positioned away from the midline, ensuring adequate skin coverage while preserving skin close to the anus.

The surgeon marks the skin nearly perpendicular to the midline, then curves the incision radially towards the ten o'clock or two o'clock position, ensuring the incision radiates away from the anus and avoids tension points.

The surgeon uses a pen to draw an incision line radiating from the anus, following a contour that approaches the right safety line. Marcane with epinephrine is administered to minimize bleeding. The incision is made through the damaged skin down to the subcutaneous tissue.

The surgeon incises the skin flap out to the safety lines, applying upward traction to avoid buttonholing the flap. The skin is undermined and detached from the superficial external sphincter fibers, allowing the deep skin tethered in the cleft to rotate away. The underlying soft tissue naturally falls together, forming a pad to prevent the skin from returning to the cleft.

The surgeon undermines the flap of skin to be excised out to the safety lines, keeping the flap thin to preserve adequate soft tissue in the cleft. This technique ensures that the remaining soft tissue will naturally fall together once the tapes are removed.
The surgeon sweeps away granulation tissue lining the pilonidal cavity, revealing healthy, well-vascularized scar tissue beneath.

The surgeon makes an incision to release tension and minimize the defect, avoiding excision. Dense scar tissue is visible, which will aid in approximating soft tissue and closing the dead space. The tapes are then removed.

The surgeon removes the tapes from the buttocks to release tension, allowing for a tension-free repair. The preserved skin flap is gently draped across the midline to the safety line on the opposite side, ensuring it reaches comfortably without tension. The flap of skin on the contralateral side is then excised.

The surgeon places a drain deep in the wound and closes the soft tissue over it, using closed suction drains or vessel loops. The midline soft tissue is approximated with PDS sutures in multiple layers, with twenty to thirty sutures used to prevent seroma and hematoma formation, visibly bringing the soft tissue together.

The surgeon places interrupted PDS sutures starting from the depth of the wound and working outward, using approximately twenty to thirty sutures to approximate the dead space and prevent hematoma and seroma formation.
The surgeon places drains either deep near the sacrum or just beneath the flap after approximating the cleft, ensuring proper drainage post-surgery.

Near the anus, the surgeon places a dermal stitch between the side wall where the skin rotates significantly. Additional subcutaneous and dermal stitches are placed to ensure proper orientation of the cleft before closing the skin.
The surgeon performs a subcuticular closure using monocryl sutures to approximate the skin edges. Proline sutures are also mentioned as an alternative for achieving similar results.

The cleft appears flat in two dimensions, with the patient's relaxed muscles contributing to its flattened appearance. The raw skin, previously deep in the cleft and midline, is now visibly shifted to the right. Incisions radiating away from the anus are observed, similar to those in hemorrhoidectomy procedures.
